title: Customs and Traditions
overview: British customs and traditions blend history with everyday life. From royal ceremonies to local festivals, these shared experiences strengthen national identity. Public holidays, seasonal festivals, and family customs give people a sense of continuity, belonging, and pride.

study_sections.heading: National Events
study_sections.content: Trooping the Colour celebrates monarch's official birthday. Bonfire Night (5 Nov) marks Gunpowder Plot 1605. Remembrance Day (11 Nov) honours war dead with red poppies. May Day celebrates spring with fairs and dancing.

study_sections.heading: Religious Festivals
study_sections.content: Christmas Day (25 Dec) celebrates Christ's birth. Easter Sunday marks resurrection. New Year's Eve (31 Dec) celebrated with fireworks. Hogmanay is Scotland's New Year celebration.

study_sections.heading: Patron Saints' Days
study_sections.content: St George's Day (England) - 23 April. St Andrew's Day (Scotland) - 30 November. St David's Day (Wales) - 1 March. St Patrick's Day (Northern Ireland) - 17 March.

study_sections.heading: Local Traditions
study_sections.content: Highland Games in Scotland feature tug-of-war and caber tossing. Traditional foods include roast dinner (Sunday lunch) and fish and chips. Tea is iconic British drink. Boxing Day (26 Dec) follows Christmas. Public holidays called bank holidays.

key_facts: Bank holidays are public holidays in the UK, including Christmas Day (25 December) and Boxing Day (26 December).
Easter takes place in spring and celebrates the resurrection of Jesus with services, family meals and egg traditions.
Bonfire Night on 5 November marks the failure of the 1605 Gunpowder Plot with fireworks and bonfires.
Remembrance Sunday in November honours those who died in war, symbolised by red poppies.
Patron saint days—St David (1 March), St Patrick (17 March), St George (23 April) and St Andrew (30 November)—celebrate each nation of the UK.
Hogmanay and Burns Night (25 January) are important Scottish celebrations; Highland Games showcase Scottish sport and music.
Notting Hill Carnival celebrates Caribbean culture; other major festivals include Diwali, Eid, Hanukkah, Vaisakhi and Chinese New Year.
Traditional foods such as the Sunday roast, fish and chips and afternoon tea remain popular.
Trooping the Colour marks the monarch’s official birthday with a parade in June.
Village fêtes, agricultural shows and summer festivals bring communities together across the UK.
memory_hook: Picture the UK year as a circular calendar: fireworks in November, winter feasts, spring saint days, summer fêtes and carnival colours—traditions old and new woven through every season.
